relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Joshua's age is named, and the unfinished work is allowed to remain unfinished by him.
The remaining land begins with peoples who are still present.
Philistine cities and Avvim remain outside Israel's control.
Canaanite and Sidonian territories continue across the south and coast.
Gebal and Lebanon extend the unpossessed horizon.
A future promise of dispossession is spoken over living hill-country communities.
Joshua is told to allot land before every boundary is controlled.
The eastern tribes already carry inheritances given through Moses.
Arnon, Medeba, and Dibon begin the eastern map.
Sihon's former cities define the Ammonite edge.
Gilead, Hermon, and Bashan widen the territory.
Og's kingdom is remembered through defeat and Rephaim lore.
Geshur and Maacath remain, proving the land is socially mixed.
Levi receives no territorial portion but depends upon sacred service.
Reuben's inheritance is now named clan by clan.
Its border begins at Arnon and Medeba's plateau.
Heshbon and neighboring plateau cities are listed.
Jahaz, Kedemoth, and Mephaath enter the register.
More towns are placed along valley and hill.
Beth-peor, Pisgah, and Beth-jeshimoth complete another section.
Sihon's kingdom and Midianite chiefs are folded into the victory memory.
Balaam appears one last time among those killed.
Jordan closes Reuben's inheritance.
Gad's clan-based allotment begins.
Jazer, Gilead, and land near Rabbah define its reach.
Heshbon, Mizpeh, Mahanaim, and Debir continue the border.
Valley towns and Chinnereth finish Gad's territory.
The register closes Gad's cities and villages.
Half Manasseh receives its eastern share.
Bashan and sixty settlements fill the allocation.
Machir's descendants receive Gilead and Og's royal cities.
The chapter identifies these as Moses' distributions in Moab.
Levi's inheritance is named as YHWH rather than territory.

contemplative_note: Joshua 13 begins with a liberating admission: one life does not finish every sacred task. Yet the allotment map is drawn over lands where other peoples still live. Wisdom means accepting incomplete leadership without treating future possession as permission to erase present communities.
